content.text: , the right approach is to treat tools as
first-class message parts and workflow states
, not as hidden backend implementation details. In Claude apps, that matters because Anthropic’s tool-use model is explicitly structured: Claude may return
blocks, your application executes client tools or Anthropic executes server tools, and then the interaction continues with
data. So the UI should expose that lifecycle clearly instead of flattening everything into a single assistant text bubble.

Anthropic’s docs define tool use as a loop: Claude decides whether to call a tool, emits a structured tool request, the tool is executed in your application for client tools or on Anthropic’s infrastructure for server tools, and the conversation proceeds with the result. The AI SDK chatbot docs reinforce a UI-friendly version of this idea by recommending that messages be rendered from their
string, because parts can represent text, tool invocation, tool result, files, reasoning, and other structured elements. That means the best frontend state model is

Do not model tool use as “assistant is loading.” That state is too coarse because it cannot distinguish between Claude deciding to call a tool, waiting for the tool to finish, requesting human approval, receiving a result, or resuming final answer generation. A robust Claude UI should instead track a tool lifecycle with explicit statuses.
A strong UI state model usually includes these statuses:
— the tool call has been proposed or created but not started.
— the tool needs human confirmation before it may run.
— the tool completed and returned a result.
— optional state when tool output itself streams progressively.
These states map much better to Claude’s actual tool loop than a single generic spinner.
primarily by where execution happens. Client tools run in your application and require your code to execute the operation and return a

are executed on Anthropic’s infrastructure and you receive the results directly. This difference should shape the UI because client tools often need richer local progress, permissions, and approval flows, while server tools can often be shown as simple remote actions with result summaries.
Why UI state must be explicit

If tool state is not explicit, users misread pauses as model slowness or bugs. For example, a long-running client tool might actually be doing useful work, but if the interface only shows “Claude is typing,” the user cannot tell whether the model is thinking, waiting on a network call, blocked on a permission gate, or stuck. Good tool-use UI removes that ambiguity by turning hidden backend transitions into visible product states.
The best rendering unit is usually a
tool card inside the assistant message stream
. The AI SDK docs recommend using

for rendering because parts support different message types including tool invocation and tool result, and the shadcn AI guidance in the search results similarly suggests rendering tool parts as dedicated components rather than forcing them into plain text. In practice, each tool call becomes a structured visual block with name, status, input summary, output summary, and any user actions.
Think of one assistant turn as potentially containing multiple layers:
One or more tool invocation parts.
This model is more faithful than assuming one turn equals one string.
For a simple chat app, each tool card should usually show:
Small human-readable description of what the tool is doing.
Retry or approve button when relevant.
That already gives users much better transparency than a hidden tool loop.
Approval is a special tool state and should be treated as its own first-class UI branch. The AI SDK 6 announcement in the search results specifically references approval handling in

, checking tool invocation state and returning approval responses from the UI. That means approval should not look like generic waiting; it should render as an actionable card with a clear prompt, the proposed tool action, and explicit
For anything beyond a toy app, model tool-use UI as a

, not scattered booleans. The frontend skill results recommend XState or state-machine thinking for complex state logic, and this is exactly the kind of problem where that discipline helps because tool flows can branch into approval, failure, retry, cancellation, and resume paths. Even if you do not use a formal library, you should design the states and transitions deliberately.
A practical TypeScript model might look like this:
This structure is small, but it captures the core lifecycle needed for rendering and transitions.

of message-level state and transient UI state. The durable facts such as tool name, input, output, final status, and timestamps should be part of the conversation or server-side workflow record, while presentation concerns like “expanded details panel,” “selected tab,” or “show raw JSON” should stay local to the client UI. This mirrors the broader distinction between AI state and UI state.

Do not keep one tool status in the message stream and another separate unrelated status in a global store unless you have a clear synchronization contract. The AI SDK state model already provides structured messages and parts, so your derived UI should usually read from that source and add only local presentation flags around it. Duplicate unsynchronized state is one of the easiest ways tool UIs become inconsistent.
The AI SDK docs explain that
manages streaming messages and exposes lifecycle state such as
. Those hook-level states are useful, but they describe the
, not the full internal tool lifecycle. So the right pattern is to combine chat-level streaming state with finer-grained per-tool states derived from parts, metadata, or custom stream data.
The AI SDK docs show that the server can attach

such as timestamps, model names, and token usage to streamed responses. This is very helpful for tool UIs because you can attach metadata like tool duration, execution host, approval requirement, or result counts without cluttering the visible assistant text. Then the client can show compact tool badges like “Web search · 1.2s · 3 sources.”
Structured parts are better than parsing text

Never rely on string parsing like “if assistant text contains ‘Searching...’ then show tool state.” Anthropic gives you structured tool semantics, and the AI SDK gives you structured message parts, so use those instead of trying to infer machine state from prose. Parsing text for state is brittle, localization-hostile, and hard to maintain.
A clean React decomposition often looks like this:
This keeps rendering modular and prevents every tool concern from being jammed into one chat bubble component.

Not every tool result should be displayed in full. Many results are best shown as a short human summary with an expandable raw view, because raw tool payloads can be noisy and overwhelm the chat thread. The best pattern is usually “compact by default, inspectable on demand.”
Tool failures need their own visible state, not a silent fallback into assistant text. The AI SDK docs describe

handling and retry/regenerate controls at the chat level, and the same product thinking should apply to tool cards: show that a tool failed, preserve prior completed work, and offer the correct recovery action such as retrying the tool or regenerating the assistant step. This is much more trustworthy than hiding tool errors inside a vague final apology.

for aborting an active response stream. For tool-use UIs, cancellation should ideally propagate meaningfully so the user can see whether the current assistant turn, the current tool run, or the entire workflow was stopped. If your architecture supports it, mark the tool card as
instead of leaving it forever in
The AI SDK docs note that
can trigger a render on every incoming chunk and provides

to reduce update frequency. This matters for tool-use UIs because frequent chunk updates plus complex tool cards can create expensive rerenders. Good practice is to keep derived selectors narrow, memoize heavy renderers, and throttle updates when the stream is very granular.

Use local component state for small chat apps, and add a lightweight store like Zustand only when multiple distant components must react to the same tool state. The search results emphasize that chat state can be accessed across components and that selector-based access helps prevent unnecessary rerenders. A good rule is: do not introduce a global store just because tools exist; introduce it when the rendering topology truly needs shared access.
For most Claude apps using React or Next.js, a solid production pattern is:
Render assistant turns from structured message parts, not flat strings.
Convert Claude tool calls into explicit tool invocation objects with lifecycle statuses.
Show each tool call as a dedicated UI card inside the conversation flow.
Support approval, retry, cancellation, and error presentation as explicit branches.
Persist durable tool facts in server-backed conversation state, but keep display toggles local.
This gives you both transparency and maintainability.

A strong concise explanation is: “In Claude apps, I manage tool use as structured workflow state rather than hidden loading. Claude returns explicit tool-use blocks, so I render those as first-class message parts in the UI, usually as tool cards with statuses like pending, running, awaiting approval, succeeded, and failed. I keep durable tool facts in conversation state, keep presentation flags local, and use event-driven or part-driven rendering instead of parsing assistant text. That makes tool execution transparent, debuggable, and easier for users to trust.”
